does: This paper proposes DynaCF, a dynamic reweighting framework that mitigates shortcut learning in reward models trained from pairwise preferences. The core method applies semantics-preserving counterfactual perturbations to training samples during optimization and measures how sensitive each sample is to these perturbations by tracking margin shifts and preference flips under the current model. Samples exhibiting higher shortcut sensitivity—those where model predictions change dramatically from superficial modifications—are downweighted in the Bradley-Terry objective function. This encourages the reward model to rely on task-relevant preference signals rather than exploiting spurious correlations. The authors demonstrate through extensive experiments that DynaCF consistently improves robustness in preference modeling compared to baseline approaches.
connects: This work builds on longstanding research into shortcut learning and spurious correlations in machine learning, particularly within the reward modeling literature that trains models from human preferences. It extends prior work on static shortcut detection methods by introducing dynamic, model-adaptive measurement of shortcut sensitivity during training. The approach complements existing research on robustness in preference learning and connects to broader literature on data valuation and sample reweighting strategies used to improve model generalization. The use of counterfactual perturbations relates to causal inference and interpretability methods in ML, while the Bradley-Terry objective is a standard framework in preference modeling literature. DynaCF specifically responds to the problem that reward models often exploit spurious superficial cues rather than learning genuine response quality, a critical failure mode in RLHF and preference-based learning systems.
enables: DynaCF points toward more robust and interpretable reward modeling systems that could improve alignment and safety in large language models and other systems trained via preference feedback. The dynamic measurement of shortcut sensitivity during training opens possibilities for real-time model monitoring and adaptive training procedures that adjust to emerging failure modes. Future work could extend this framework to more sophisticated counterfactual generation strategies, multi-modal perturbations, or combinations with other robustness techniques. The approach may enable better transfer of learned preferences across domains and more reliable deployment of reward models in production systems. Additionally, the method could support development of certified robustness guarantees for preference models and inform better data collection practices for human feedback, ultimately contributing to more trustworthy AI systems.
